name: Huntington Ingalls Industries, Inc.
executive_summary: Huntington Ingalls is the sole U.S. builder of nuclear aircraft carriers and a duopoly participant in nuclear submarines and Arleigh Burke destroyers, with a multi-decade naval shipbuilding backlog and a growing Mission Technologies franchise in unmanned systems and electronic warfare. The quantitative screen rates HII a strong buy on cheapness (P/E 17.7, P/B 2.1), quality (ROE 12.2%), dividend support (2.0% yield), FCF yield, and financial health (debt/equity 57%), with nine of 22 models passing across five factor families. The valuation hook is a defence-budget-aligned order book—remaining performance obligations reached $54.0 billion at 31 March 2026—trading at a modest earnings multiple after a recent share-price pullback. The central debate is whether revenue acceleration (Q1 2026 sales +13% year-on-year) can translate into sustained margin expansion, or whether shipyard execution, supply-chain delays, and contract accounting headwinds will keep profitability flat despite record backlog.
investment_thesis: For a value investor, HII offers a rare combination of statutory moat, visible contracted revenue, and screen-level undervaluation. The screen passes Graham Enterprising, FCF Yield, Neff PEGY, Quality Value, Dividend Growth, Dreman Contrarian, Piotroski F-Score, Composite Value, and Financial Health—covering cheapness, quality, dividend, GARP, and risk families with a composite score of 88% (sector-relative 81%) and high data quality (19/20 metrics). Business quality aligns with the quantitative signal: approximately 81% of revenue derives from the U.S. Navy (FY2025 10-K business description), HII holds sole-source positions in nuclear carrier construction and refuelling, and long-term contracts span Virginia-class submarines, Gerald R. Ford-class carriers, and Flight III destroyers. Mission Technologies adds optionality in REMUS UUVs, spectrum warfare (GRIMM/VIPER), and autonomous surface/undersea platforms—areas receiving incremental contract flow in 2025–26. Balance-sheet metrics support the risk-family pass: moderate leverage, rising equity, and FCF recovery to $794 million in FY2025 (Yahoo fallback; see Financial Review). The stock screens cheap relative to a defence industrial base benefiting from elevated naval spending, while paying a growing dividend ($1.38 per quarter declared in Q1 2026, per Q1 2026 10-Q). The screen’s strong-buy label is credible on asset quality and cash-generation potential; near-term earnings momentum is less clear.
financial_review:
**Annual results (10-K).** Two annual 10-K filings are indexed (FY2024, filed 6 February 2025; FY2025, filed 5 February 2026). Readable body extracts include business description and risk disclosures but truncate before Item 8 financial statement tables; annual income-statement, balance-sheet, and cash-flow figures below therefore fall back to `financials_annual.json`, stated explicitly.

| Metric | FY2023 | FY2024 | FY2025 | Source |
|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| Revenue | $11.45bn | $11.54bn | $12.48bn | Yahoo (fallback) |
| Net income | $681m | $550m | $605m | Yahoo (fallback) |
| Diluted EPS | $17.07 | $13.96 | $15.39 | Yahoo (fallback) |
| Operating income | $744m | $486m | $611m | Yahoo (fallback) |
| Free cash flow | $678m | $26m | $794m | Yahoo (fallback) |

FY2025 revenue rose approximately 8% year-on-year, with net income up 10% and a sharp FCF rebound from FY2024’s trough ($26m), driven by working-capital normalisation and lower acquisition outlays (FY2025 cash flow shows no business purchases vs. prior years). FY2023 net income remains the recent peak, reflecting the cyclicality of contract accounting and programme mix.

From the FY2024 10-K body extract (primary filing): total backlog at 31 December 2024 was approximately **$48.7 billion** versus $48.1 billion at end-2023, with management expecting roughly 21% of that backlog to convert to sales in 2025. The FY2025 10-K body extract does not include a readable backlog disclosure in the available extract—a data gap.

Balance-sheet highlights at FY2025 year-end (Yahoo fallback): total debt $2.92bn, stockholders’ equity $5.07bn, cash $774m, net debt $1.93bn; defined-benefit pension plan assets of $1.54bn are recorded on the balance sheet alongside employee benefit liabilities of $797m and current pension/OPEB obligations of $779m.

**Interim results (10-Q).** Six interim 10-Q filings are indexed with body extracts from Q2 2024 through Q1 2026.

*Q1 2026 (three months ended 31 March 2026; 10-Q filed 5 May 2026):*
- Sales and service revenues: **$3,099m** vs. $2,734m (+13%), driven by higher volumes at Newport News, Ingalls, and Mission Technologies (MD&A).
- Net earnings: **$149m** (flat year-on-year); diluted EPS **$3.79** (flat).
- Operating income: **$155m** vs. $161m (−4%).
- Segment operating income: Ingalls $49m, Newport News $88m, Mission Technologies $35m (total $172m vs. $171m prior year).
- Remaining performance obligations: **$54.0bn**; management expects ~40% recognised as revenue through 2027, ~35% through 2029, balance thereafter.
- Balance sheet (31 March 2026): total assets $12,533m; stockholders’ equity **$5,147m**; long-term debt **$2,701m**; cash **$216m** (seasonal working-capital build—net cash used in operating activities was $390m in the quarter).
- Dividend declared: $1.38 per share (vs. $1.35 prior-year quarter).

*Nine months ended 30 September 2025 (Q3 2025 10-Q):*
- Sales and service revenues: **$9,008m** vs. $8,531m (+6%).
- Net earnings: **$446m** vs. $427m (+4%); diluted EPS **$11.35** vs. $10.81.
- Operating income: **$485m** vs. $425m (+14%).
- Net cumulative catch-up contract adjustments were unfavourable: −$13m on operating income for the nine-month period (vs. −$46m prior year).

**Trend assessment.** Revenue and backlog are accelerating—RPO of $54.0bn at Q1 2026 exceeds the $48.7bn year-end 2024 backlog disclosed in the FY2024 10-K—yet near-term margins are compressing: Q1 2026 operating income fell despite double-digit revenue growth, and segment margins at Mission Technologies declined ($35m on $748m revenue vs. $40m on $735m). FY2025 FCF recovery improves the value case, but Q1 2026 cash consumption from contract assets and payables warrants monitoring. No going-concern language appears in available filing extracts.
risks_and_flags:
**Customer and programme concentration.** The FY2025 10-K states approximately 81% of 2025 revenue came from the U.S. Navy. Budget delays, continuing-resolution funding, or programme cancellations would disproportionately affect HII.

**Execution and supply chain.** The FY2025 10-K notes HII is working with the Navy to align schedules on Enterprise (CVN-80) due to late material and to assess technical baseline changes. Raw-material access and pricing challenges are flagged as ongoing. Q1 2026 MD&A cites inflationary supply-chain pressures, labour shortfalls, and workforce stoppage risk as factors affecting segment operating income.

**Quality and regulatory.** The Q1 2026 10-Q discloses that in 2024 HII identified quality issues involving noncompliance with welding procedures at Newport News, notified the U.S. Government, and **cannot yet predict or reasonably estimate the ultimate outcome**. This sits alongside standing DCAA/DCMA audit risk, potential debarment, and contract payment withholding for business-system deficiencies described in the FY2025 10-K.

**Contract accounting.** Unfavourable cumulative catch-up revenue adjustments reduced nine-month 2025 operating income by $13m (Q3 2025 10-Q). Large Requests for Equitable Adjustment and dispute resolution are routine in naval shipbuilding.

**Pension and OPEB.** Legacy defined-benefit obligations remain material: pension plan liabilities of $155m and other postretirement liabilities of $195m on the Q1 2026 balance sheet, with ongoing FAS/CAS adjustment volatility (Q1 2026: −$9m operating FAS/CAS adjustment).

**Labour relations.** Approximately 45% of ~44,000 employees are unionised across 13 collective bargaining agreements (Q1 2026 10-Q). Ingalls agreements expire March 2031 per the Q1 filing; any future dispute could disrupt production.

**Competition and strategic.** General Dynamics competes across shipbuilding; the FY2025 10-K warns that if a government shipyard performed carrier refuelling, HII’s market position would be materially affected. In 2025, HII agreed with the U.S. Coast Guard to terminate production of the 11th National Security Cutter.

**Legal/contingent.** Ongoing asbestos-related claims, Venezuela-related litigation (XBRL tags in FY2025 10-K), and environmental remediation accruals under EPA Potentially Responsible Party designations. No debt covenant breach or going-concern qualification identified in available extracts.
news_highlights:
News coverage over the past year is substantial (50 articles in `news_manifest.json` from July 2025–July 2026); not thin.

**Contract wins and strategy (2026):**
- “HII Stock In The Spotlight As Huntington Wins $418M Naval Contract” (Stocktwits, 22 June 2026).
- “Huntington Ingalls Industries (HII) Wins Tradewinds Status, Is The Stock Still A Bargain?” (Yahoo Finance, 9 July 2026)—GRIMM/VIPER electronic warfare products achieve awardable status on CDAO Tradewinds marketplace.
- “Huntington Ingalls Industries (HII) Wins Defense Awards And Expands Autonomous Vessel Production” (Yahoo Finance, 8 July 2026)—Lionfish UUV production contract; ROMULUS USV development.
- “Is Huntington Ingalls Expanding Its Presence in the UUV Market?” (Yahoo Finance, 9 July 2026).

**Earnings and valuation debate:**
- “HII Reports First Quarter 2026 Results” (GlobeNewswire, 5 May 2026); “HII Q1 2026 slides: revenue surges 13% as margins compress” (Investing.com, 11 May 2026).
- “Huntington Ingalls (HII) Q4 Earnings and Revenues Top Estimates” (Yahoo Finance, 5 February 2026).
- “Huntington Ingalls Industries (HII) Stock Looks Cheap On Cash Flow And Earnings” (Yahoo Finance, 8 July 2026).
- “HII Maintains Buy Rating but TD Cowen Lowers Price Target to $36” (GuruFocus, 13 July 2026).

**Capital allocation and governance:**
- “Huntington Ingalls Industries (NYSE:HII) Has Announced That It Will Be Increasing Its Dividend To $1.38” (simplywall.st, 30 October 2025).
- “Have Insiders Sold Huntington Ingalls Industries Shares Recently?” (simplywall.st, 31 May 2026); “Huntington Ingalls EVP Chewning sells $736k in stock” (Investing.com, 5 March 2026).

**International expansion:**
- “US shipyard HII expands in UK to service growing Europe UUV business” (Defense News, 13 January 2026).
- “UK industry joins US Navy Virginia-class attack submarine programme” (Navy Lookout, 13 May 2026).

**Macro/defence spending:**
- “Trump Just Made An Obscure Pentagon Office Bigger Than The Marine Corps…” (Yahoo Finance, 7 July 2026)—references elevated autonomous-warfare budget proposals relevant to Mission Technologies.
research_rationale: Deep research largely confirms the quantitative strong-buy case on moat, backlog growth, balance-sheet quality, and FCF recovery, but moderates conviction owing to flat Q1 2026 earnings despite revenue acceleration, unresolved Newport News welding compliance matters, and persistent contract-accounting and supply-chain execution risks not fully captured by value screens.
